topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Professor Albright was leaving. The words swam before my eyes, blurring the screen until the familiar font seemed alien and mocking. I reread the subject line three times, each repetition scraping a dull ache against my ribs. I stood up abruptly, the cheap plastic chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor of the deserted library study room. My hands trembled, and I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically stop the sudden, hot sting behind my lids. The silence of the building, usually a comforting blanket of focused concentration, now felt cavernous and hostile. I walked to the window, staring out at the grey expanse of the campus quad. Every familiar landmark seemed suddenly muted, stripped of the vibrancy that had anchored my entire academic existence. A low, involuntary sound escaped my throat—a soft, choked gasp—and I quickly swallowed, the dryness in my mouth making the effort painful.

[story 2]

I found myself pacing my small dorm room, the worn carpet absorbing the sound of my hurried footsteps. The news about Dr. Evans’ departure had settled in my chest like a heavy, cold stone. I picked up my laptop, intending to look at the outline for Chapter Three, but the screen remained stubbornly blank. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, incapable of forming coherent sentences. A sudden, overwhelming urge to cry surfaced, sharp and immediate, but I fought it down, focusing instead on the meticulous arrangement of my textbooks on the desk. I traced the spine of a well-loved volume, feeling a strange, hollow emptiness spread through my core. When I finally forced myself to speak, the words came out thin and reedy. "It’s just… unexpected," I managed, the tone devoid of any real inflection, a brittle shell protecting something much softer and much more fragile underneath.

[story 3]

The coffee tasted like ash. I stared at the lukewarm mug, the steam long dissipated, mirroring the fading warmth I felt inside. When the announcement finally registered, it wasn't a sudden shock, but a slow, creeping realization that the scaffolding supporting my entire project was being dismantled. I slumped onto the edge of my desk, the wood cool beneath my forearms. My shoulders began to shake almost imperceptibly, a subtle vibration of distress that I tried to mask by gripping the edge of the desk so tightly my knuckles turned white. I closed my eyes, trying to summon the logical part of my brain to process the logistics—who would take over, what were the next steps—but the thoughts kept dissolving into a dull, pervasive ache. I let out a long, slow exhale, a sound that held all the unsaid weight of weeks of shared intellectual struggle.
